Prior to departure an aeroplane is loaded with 16 500 litres of fuel at a fuel density of 780 kg/m3. This is entered into the load sheet as 16500kg and... result of this error, the aeroplane is:
options: lighter than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too low.
lighter than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too high.
heavier than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too low.
heavier than anticipated and the calculated safety speeds will be too high.

questions.questionText: Fuel loaded onto an aeroplane is 15400 kg but is erroneously entered into the load and trim sheet as 14500 kg. This error is not detected by the flight crew but they will notice that:
options: speed at un-stick will be higher than expected.
\(V_{1}\) will be reached sooner than expected.
the aeroplane will rotate much earlier than expected.

questions.questionText: A pilot has finished the pre-planning. Shortly before departure an additional container with a mass of 3500 kg of traffic load is accepted. The pilot forgets to calculate the new performance value of the aircraft. Take-off and climb is accomplished without any problems. How will the performance of the aircraft due to the additional mass be affected?
options: Angle of climb and obstacle clearance will be improved.
Fuel flow and specific range will be increased.
Rate of climb and range will be decreased.
Fuel flow and endurance will be reduced.

questions.questionText: At maximum certificated Take-off Mass, an aeroplane departs from an airfield which is not limiting for either take-off or Landing Masses. During initial climb the smaller one engine suffers a contained disintegration. An emergency is declared and the aeroplane returns to departure airfield for an immediate landing. The most likely result of this action will be:
options: a high threshold speed and possible undercarriage or other structural failure.
a high threshold speed and a shorter stop distance.
a landing short resultant from the increased angle of approach due to the very high aeroplane mass.
a landing further along the runway than normal.

questions.questionText: During a violent avoidance manoeuvre, a light twin aircraft certified to EASA CS-23 requirements, was subjected to an... is certified in the normal category for a load factor of -1.9 to +3.8. Considering the certification requirements and taking into account... conception, a supplementary margin in the flight envelope, it might be possible to observe:
options: no distortion, permanent or temporary of the structure.
elastic deformation whilst the load was applied, but no permanent distortion.
rupture of one or more structural components.

questions.questionText: If an aeroplane is at a higher mass than anticipated, for a given airspeed the angle of attack will:
options: remain constant, drag will increase and endurance will increase.
be decreased, drag will decrease and endurance will increase.
remain constant, drag will decrease and endurance will decrease.
be greater, drag will increase and endurance will decrease.

questions.questionText: Overloading has the following effects on performance:
options: reduced take-off and landing distance, increased VNE and increased fuel consumption.
reduced take-off and landing distance, increased VNE and reduced rate of climb.
increased take-off and landing distance, reduced rate of climb and increased fuel consumption.
increased take-off and landing distance, increased rate of climb and increased fuel consumption.

questions.questionText: An additional baggage container is loaded into the aft cargo compartment but is not entered into the load and trim sheet. The aeroplane will be heavier than expected and calculated take-off...
options: are unaffected but V1 will be increased.

questions.questionText: In order to provide an adequate buffet boundary at the commencement of the cruise a speed of 1.3 Vs is used. At a mass of 120000 kg this is a CAS of 180 kts. If the mass of the service is increased to... (approx 135k based on 191 kts):
options: unaffected as Vs always occurs at the same angle of attack.
increased to 191 kts, drag will decrease and air distance per kg of fuel will increase.
increased to 191 kts, drag will increase and air distance per kg of fuel will decrease.
increased to 202 kts but, since the same angle of attack is used, drag and range will remain the same.

questions.questionText: In cruise flight, an aft centre of gravity location will:
options: not change the static curve of stability into longitudinal.
does not influence longitudinal static stability.

questions.questionText: What determines the longitudinal stability of an aeroplane?
options: The relationship of thrust and lift to weight and drag.
The effectiveness of the horizontal stabilizer, rudder and rudder trim tab.
The location of the centre of gravity with respect to the neutral point.
The dihedral, angle of sweepback and the keel effect.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The lowest stalling speed is obtained if the actual centre of gravity is located in the middle between the aft and forward limit of centre of gravity.
If the actual centre of gravity is close to the forward limit of the centre of gravity the aeroplane may be unstable, making it necessary to increase elevator forces.
A tail heavy aeroplane is less stable and stalls at a lower speed than a nose heavy aeroplane.
If the actual centre of gravity is located behind the aft limit of centre of gravity it is possible that the aeroplane will be unstable, making it necessary to increase elevator forces.

questions.questionText: When the centre of gravity is at the forward limit, an aeroplane will be:
options: extremely unstable and require excessive elevator control to change pitch.
extremely unstable and require small elevator control to change pitch.
extremely stable and require small elevator control to change pitch.
extremely stable and will require excessive elevator control to change pitch.

questions.questionText: Explain the effect of a centre of gravity (CG) which is in front of the forward limit:
options: The range decreases and the absolute ceiling increases.
The range increases and the absolute ceiling decreases.
Both the range and the absolute ceiling decrease.
Both the range and the absolute ceiling increase.

questions.questionText: For a given configuration, the stall speed of an aeroplane will be highest when loaded to a:
options: maximum allowable mass with the most aft CG.
maximum allowable mass with the most forward CG.
low gross mass with the most forward CG.
low gross mass with the most aft CG.

questions.questionText: If nose wheel moves aft during gear retraction, how will this movement affect the location of the centre of gravity (CG) on the aircraft?
options: It will cause the CG to move aft.
It will not affect the CG location.
It will cause the CG to move forward.
The CG location will change, but the direction cannot be told from the information given.

questions.questionText: The effect of the centre of gravity (CG) being close to the forward limit is:
options: a reduced rate of climb capability.
a reduction in the specific fuel consumption.
a better rate of climb capability.
a decrease in the induced drag.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The station (STA) is always the location of the centre of gravity in relation to a reference point, normally the leading edge of the wing.
The centre of gravity is given in percent of MAC calculated from the leading edge of the wing, where MAC always = the wing chord halfway between the centre line of the fuselage and the wing tip.
A tail heavy aeroplane is less stable and stalls at a lower speed than a nose heavy aeroplane.
If the actual centre of gravity is located behind the aft limit the aeroplane longitudinal stability increases.

questions.questionText: If the centre of gravity is near the forward limit, the aeroplane will:
options: require elevator trim, which will result in an increase in fuel consumption.
require less power for a given airspeed.
benefit from reduced drag due to the decrease in angle of attack.
tend to over rotate during take-off.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is most likely to affect the range of centre of gravity positions on an aeroplane?
options: The need to minimize drag forces and so improve efficiency.
The need to maintain a low value of stalling speed.
Elevator and tailplane (horizontal stabilizer) effectiveness in all flight conditions.

questions.questionText: The Operating Mass (OM) equals:
options: empty mass plus the take-off fuel mass.
dry operating mass plus the take-off fuel mass.
empty mass plus the operating items.
empty mass plus the trip fuel mass.

questions.questionText: Conversion of fuel volume to mass:
options: must be done by using actual measured fuel density values.
must be done using fuel density values of 0.79 for JP 1 and 0.76 for JP 4 as specified in EASA AIR OPS.
must be done by using standard fuel density values as specified in EASA AIR OPS.
may be done by using standard fuel density values as specified by the Operations Manual, if the actual fuel density is not known.

questions.questionText: Basic Empty Mass (BEM) is:
options: Dry Operating Mass minus Traffic Load.
Dry Operating Mass minus fuel load.
a component of Dry Operating Mass.
the actual Take-off Mass, minus Traffic Load.

questions.questionText: The maximum quantity of fuel that can be loaded into an aircraft's tanks is given as 400 US gallons. If the fuel density (specific gravity) is given as 0.79 the mass of fuel which may be loaded is:

The Take-off Mass (TOM) of an aeroplane is 66 700 kg which includes a Traffic Load (TL) of 14 200 kg and a usable fuel load at the start of the take-off run of 10 500 kg. If the standard mass for the crew is 545 kg the Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is:
By adding to the Basic Empty Mass (BEM) the following fixed necessary equipment for a specific flight (catering, safety and rescue equipment, fly away kit, crew), we get:
Which statement regarding the relationship between traffic load and range is correct?
options: The maximum traffic load is not limited by the reserve fuel quantity.
The maximum zero fuel mass limits the maximum quantity of fuel.
The Maximum Landing Mass is basically equal to the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass.
The traffic load can be limited by the desired range.

questions.questionText: With respect to aeroplane loading in the planning phase, which of the following statements is always correct?
options: MTOM = ZFM + maximum possible fuel mass

questions.questionText: The Maximum Certificated Taxi (or Ramp) Mass is that mass to which an aeroplane may be loaded prior to engine start. It is:
options: a fixed value which is listed in the Flight Manual.
a value which varies with airfield temperature and altitude. Corrections are listed in the Flight Manual.
a value which is only affected by the outside air temperature. Corrections are calculated from data given in the Flight Manual.
a value which varies only with airfield altitude. Standard corrections are listed in the Flight Manual.

questions.questionText: Considering only structural limitations, on long distance flights (at the aeroplane maximum range), the Traffic Load is normally limited by:
options: the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass plus the Take-off Mass.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is:
options: a structural limit listed in the Flight Manual as a fixed value.
governed by the Traffic Load to be carried.
tabulated in the Flight Manual against arguments of airfield elevation and temperature.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The Maximum Take-off Mass is equal to the maximum mass when leaving the ramp.
The Basic Empty Mass is equal to the mass of the aeroplane excluding Traffic Load and usable fuel but including the crew.
The Maximum Zero Fuel Mass ensures that the centre of gravity remains within limits after the uplift of fuel.
The Maximum Landing Mass of an aeroplane is restricted by structural limitations, performance limitations and the strength of the runway.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane, which is scheduled to fly an oceanic sector, is due to depart from a high altitude airport in the tropics at 14:00 local time. The airport has an exceptionally long runway. Which of the following is most likely to be the limiting factor(s) in determining the Take-off Mass?
options: Altitude and temperature of the departure airfield.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Certificated Take-off Mass is:
options: a structural limit which may not be exceeded for any take-off.
a take-off limiting mass which is affected by the aerodrome altitude and temperature.
limited by the runway take-off distance available. It is tabulated in the Flight Manual.
a take-off limiting mass which is governed by the gradient of climb after reaching V...

questions.questionText: The Maximum Taxi (Ramp) Mass is governed by:
options: bearing strength of the taxiway pavement.

questions.questionText: The Maximum Structural Take-off Mass is:
options: a limit which may not be exceeded for any take-off.
limited by the take-off distance available. It is tabulated in the flight manual.
a take-off limiting mass which is affected by the aerodrome altitude and temperature.
a take-off limiting mass which is governed by the gradient of climb after reaching VY.

questions.questionText: The maximum mass to which an aeroplane may be loaded, prior to engine start, is:
Considering only structural limitations, on very short legs with minimum take-off fuel, the Traffic Load is normally limited by:
For a particular aeroplane, the structural maximum mass without any fuel on board, other than unusable quantities, is:
options: a fixed value which will limit the amount of fuel carried.
a variable value which is governed by the payload carried.
a fixed value which is stated in the Aeroplane Operating Manual.
a variable value which may limit the payload carried.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane weighing schedule indicates that the Empty Mass is 57 320 kg. The nominal Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is 60120 kg and the Maximum Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) is given as 72100 kg. Which of the following is a correct statement in relation to this aeroplane?
options: Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Useful Load is 11 980 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Useful Load is 14 780 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Traffic Load for this aeroplane is 14 780 kg.
Operational items have a mass of 2 800 kg and the maximum Traffic Load for this aeroplane is 11 980 kg.

questions.questionText: The medium range twin jet transport is scheduled to operate from a departure airfield where conditions limit the Take-off Mass to 65 050 kg. The destination airfield has a performance limited Landing Mass of 54 500 kg. The Dry Operating Mass is 34 900 kg. Loading data is as follows: Taxi Fuel: 350 kg; Trip Fuel: 9250 kg; Contingency and final reserve fuel: 1100 kg; Alternate Fuel: 1000 kg; Traffic Load: 18600 kg. Check the load and ensure that the flight may be operated without exceeding any of the aeroplane limits.
options: The aircraft may be safely operated with the stated Traffic and Fuel Load.
The aircraft is Zero Fuel Mass limited and the Traffic Load must be reduced to 14 170 kg.
The aircraft is Landing Mass limited and the Traffic Load must be reduced to 17 500 kg.
The aircraft may be safely operated with an additional 200 kg of Traffic Load.

questions.questionText: Given: Basic Empty Mass: 1764 lbs; Optional Equipment: 35 lbs; Pilot + Passenger: 300 lbs; Cargo: 350 lbs; Ramp Fuel (Block Fuel): 60 Gal; Trip Fuel: 35 Gal; Taxi Fuel: 1.7 Gal; Final Reserve Fuel: 18 Gal; Fuel density: 6 lbs/Gal. What is the expected landing mass (LM)?

Given: Dry Operating mass: 85 000 kg; Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 127 000 kg; Performance Limited Landing Mass: 98 500 kg; Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 89 800 kg; Fuel requirements for flight: Trip Fuel: 29 300 kg; Contingency and final reserve fuel: 3 600 kg; Alternate Fuel: 2 800 kg. The maximum Traffic Load (TL) that can be carried on this flight is:
Standard mass as used in the computation of passenger load establish the mass of a child as:
options: 35 kg irrespective of age provided they occupy a seat.
35 kg for children over 2 years occupying a seat and 10 kg for infants (less than 2 years) occupying a seat.
35 kg only if they are over 2 years old and occupy a seat.
35 kg for children over 2 years occupying a seat and 10 kg for infants (less than 2 years) not occupying a seat.

questions.questionText: The Dry Operating Mass (DOM) of an aircraft is 2 000 kg. The Maximum Take-off Mass (MTOM), Landing (MLM) and Zero Fuel Mass (MZFM) are identical at 3 500 kg. The Block Fuel Mass is 550 kg, and the Taxi Fuel Mass is 50 kg. The available mass of payload is:

Given: Dry Operating Mass: 38 000 kg; Maximum Structural Take-off Mass: 72 000 kg; Maximum Landing Mass: 65 000 kg; Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 61 000 kg; Fuel burn: 8 000 kg; Take-off Fuel: 10 300 kg. The maximum allowed Take-off Mass (TOM) and Traffic Load (TL) are respectively:

An aeroplane is to depart from an airfield at a Take-off Mass (TOM) of 302 550 kg. Fuel on board at take-off (including contingency and alternate of 19 450 kg) is 121 450 kg. The Dry Operating Mass (DOM) is 161 450 kg. The Useful Load (UL) will be:

The following data is extracted from an aeroplane loading manifest: Take-off Mass (Performance Limited): 93 500 kg; Expected Landing Mass at destination: 81 700 kg; Maximum Certificated Landing Mass: 86 300 kg; Take-off Fuel on board: 16 500 kg. During the flight a diversion is made to an en-route alternate which is not performance limited for landing. Fuel remaining at landing is 10 300 kg. The Landing Mass:
options: must be reduced to 81 700 kg in order to avoid a high speed approach.
is 87 300 kg and excess structural stress could result.

questions.questionText: Maximum Ramp Mass: 69900 kg; Maximum Take-off Mass: 69300 kg; Maximum Landing Mass: 58900 kg; Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 52740 kg; Dry Operating Mass: 34900 kg; Trip Fuel: 11800 kg; Taxi Fuel: 500 kg; Contingency and final reserve fuel: 7600 kg; Alternate Fuel: 7900 kg. Take-off and Landing Mass are not performance limited. The maximum Traffic Load (TL) that can be carried:
options: 17 840 kg (Limited by MZFM, but then TOM exceeded, so actually limited to ~7100 kg)

questions.questionText: The Maximum Take-off Mass is:

The following data applies to an aeroplane which is about to take-off: Certified Maximum Take-off Mass: 141 500 kg; Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 137 300 kg; Dry Operating Mass: 58 400 kg; Crew and crew hand baggage mass: 640 kg; Crew baggage in hold: 110 kg; Fuel on board: 60 700 kg. From this data calculate the mass of the Useful Load (UL).

Dry Operating Mass: 34 900 kg; Performance Limited Take-off Mass: 66 300 kg; Performance Limited Landing Mass: 55 200 kg; Maximum Zero Fuel Mass: 53 070 kg; Fuel requirements: Taxi Fuel: 400 kg; Trip Fuel: 8 600 kg; Contingency Fuel: 430 kg; Alternate Fuel: 970 kg; Holding Fuel: 900 kg; Traffic Load: 16 600 kg. Fuel costs at the departure airfield are such that it is decided to load the maximum fuel quantity possible. The total fuel which may be safely loaded at the parking gate prior to departure is:

The operator of an aircraft equipped with 50 seats uses standard masses for passengers and baggage. During the preparation of a scheduled flight a group of passengers present themselves at the check-in desk, it is apparent that even the lightest of these exceeds the value of the declared standard mass.
options: The operator should determine the actual mass of such passengers by weighing or by adding an adequate mass increment.
The operator is obliged to use the actual masses of each passenger.
The operator may use the standard masses for the load and balance calculation without correction.
The operator may use the standard masses for the balance but must correct these for the load calculation.

questions.questionText: In determining the Dry Operating Mass of an aeroplane it is common practice to use standard mass values for crew. These values are:
options: flight crew (male) 88 kg (female) 75 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These do not include an allowance for hand baggage.
flight crew (male) 88 kg (female) 75 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These include an allowance for hand baggage.
flight crew 85 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These do not include a hand baggage allowance.
flight crew 85 kg, cabin crew 75 kg each. These are inclusive of a hand baggage allowance.

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity of a body is that point:
options: where the sum of the moments from the external forces acting on the body is equal to zero.
which is always used as datum when computing moments.
through which the sum of the forces of all masses of the body is considered to act.
where the sum of the external forces is equal to zero.

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity of an aircraft:
options: must be maintained in a fixed position by careful distribution of the load.
may only be moved if permitted by the regulating authority and endorsed in the aircraft's certificate of airworthiness.
can be allowed to move between defined limits.
is in a fixed position and is unaffected by aircraft loading.

questions.questionText: The weight of an aircraft, which is in level non accelerated flight, is said to act:
options: always along the vertical axis of the aeroplane.
vertically through the centre of pressure.
vertically through the centre of gravity.

questions.questionText: A load placed aft of the datum:
options: has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive moment.
has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative moment but a positive mass.
has a positive arm and therefore generates a positive moment but negative mass.
has a negative arm and therefore generates a negative moment and mass.

questions.questionText: A location in the aircraft which is identified by a number designating its distance from the datum is known as:
The datum used for balance calculations is:
options: chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aircraft and necessarily situated between the leading edge and trailing edge of the wing.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aircraft, and always at the firewall level.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aeroplane, and necessarily situated between the nose and the tail of the aircraft.
chosen on the longitudinal axis of the aeroplane, but not necessarily between the nose and the tail of the aircraft.

questions.questionText: With reference to mass and balance calculations (on an aeroplane) a datum point is used. This datum point is:
options: the point through which the sum of the mass values (of the aeroplane and its contents) is assumed to act vertically.
a fixed point from which all Balance Arms are measured. It may be located anywhere on the aeroplane's longitudinal axis or on the extensions to that axis.
a point from which all Balance Arms are measured. The location of this point varies with the distribution of loads on the aeroplane.
a point near the centre of the aeroplane. It moves longitudinally as masses are added forward and aft of its location.

questions.questionText: Imagine a 5 m long lever. The lever is suspended by a pivot which is located at a distance of 3 m from one end. If a force of 600 N is pulling down on the long end of the lever, which force is necessary on the short end for obtaining equilibrium?

A mass of 600 kg is loaded at a station which is located 12 metres behind the present centre of gravity and 18 metres behind the datum. The moment for that mass used in the loading manifest is (Assume: g=10 m/s2):
Refer to figure 031-03. Given: Force Fa: 300 N; Force Fb: 150 N; Distance A: 3 m. Calculate distance B to obtain equilibrium.

A 3 m long plank is positioned on top of a pointed pivot at the plank's middle point. On the plank's left end side a mass of 1 kg is suspended and on its right end side a 2 kg mass is suspended. In which direction and by how much does the plank have to move to keep the two sides in balance?
An aircraft has its centre of gravity located 7 metres from the datum line and it has a force of 49 000 N. The moment about the datum is:
Refer to figure 031-05. Given: Force Fa: 150 N; Distance A: 6 m; Distance B: 3 m. Calculate force Fb to obtain equilibrium.
Refer to figure 031-05. For the following boom to be in balance:

At a given mass the CG position is at 15% MAC. If the leading edge of MAC is at a position 625.6 inches aft of the datum and the MAC is given as 134.5 inches determine the position of the CG in relation to the datum.
Longitudinal CG location is normally expressed:
options: as a percentage of the MAC from its trailing edge.
with respect to the centre of pressure.
as a percentage of the MAC from its leading edge.
with respect to the neutral point.

questions.questionText: The loaded centre of gravity (CG) of an aeroplane is 713 mm aft of datum. The Mean Aerodynamic Chord lies between station 524 mm aft and 1706 mm aft. The CG expressed as % MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord) is:
Refer to figure 031-19. Referring to the Loading Manual for the transport aeroplane, the maximum load intensity for the forward cargo compartment is:
options: 3305 kg in forward compartment and 4187 kg in aft compartment.
7288 kg in forward compartment and 9232 kg in aft compartment.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 031-18. For the medium range twin jet the datum point is located:
options: 540 inches forward of the front spar.
540 cm forward of the front spar.
on the nose of the aeroplane.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 031-26. An aeroplane has a Landing Mass of 53 000 kg. The range of safe CG positions, as determined from the appropriate graph in the Loading Manual, is:
options: forward limit 7.8% MAC, aft limit 27.0% MAC.
forward limit 8.2% MAC, aft limit 27.0% MAC.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane has a Mean Aerodynamic Chord (MAC) of 134.5 inches. The leading edge of this chord is at a distance of 526.6 inches aft of the datum. Give the location of the centre of gravity of the aeroplane in terms of percentage MAC if the mass of the aeroplane is acting vertically through a Balance Arm located 560 inches aft of the datum.
Refer to figure 031-26. The aeroplane has a Take-off Mass of 58 000 kg. At this mass the range of safe CG positions, as determined from the appropriate graph in the Loading Manual, is:
options: forward limit 8.5% MAC, aft limit 26.1% MAC.
forward limit 8.0% MAC, aft limit 26.5% MAC.

questions.questionText: Calculate the centre of gravity in % MAC (Mean Aerodynamic Chord) with following data: Distance from datum to CG: 12.53 m; Distance from datum to leading edge: 9.63 m; Length of MAC: 8 m.
An aeroplane has a Landing Mass of 56 000 kg. The range of safe CG positions, as determined from the appropriate graph in the Loading Manual, is:
options: forward limit 7.8% MAC, aft limit 27.0% MAC.
forward limit 8.7% MAC, aft limit 26.8% MAC.
forward limit 7.3% MAC, aft limit 26.8% MAC.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is unlikely to have any effect on the position of the centre of gravity on an aeroplane in flight?
options: Changing the tailplane (horizontal stabiliser) incidence angle.
Normal consumption of fuel for a swept wing aeroplane.
Movement of cabin attendants going about their normal duties.

questions.questionText: At the Maximum Landing Mass the range of safe CG positions, as determined from the appropriate graph in the Loading Manual, is:
options: forward limit 8.0% MAC, aft limit 27.2% MAC.
forward limit 8.0% MAC, aft limit 26.8% MAC.
forward limit 7.4% MAC, aft limit 27.0% MAC.

questions.questionText: The centre of gravity of an aeroplane is at 25% of the Mean Aerodynamic Chord. This means that the centre of gravity of the aeroplane is situated at 25% of the length of:
options: the Mean Aerodynamic Chord in relation to the trailing edge.
the Mean Aerodynamic Chord in relation to the datum.
the aeroplane in relation to the leading edge.
the Mean Aerodynamic Chord in relation to the leading edge.

questions.questionText: Select the correct statement for the CG safe range:
options: The safe range falls between the front and rear CG limits but only includes the fwd limit.
The safe range falls between the front and rear CG limits but does not include them.
The safe range falls between the front and rear CG limits but only includes the aft limit.
The safe range falls between the front and rear CG limits and includes both limits.

questions.questionText: An aeroplane must be re-weighed at certain intervals. Where an operator uses fleet masses and provided that changes have been correctly documented, this interval is:
options: whenever the Certificate of Airworthiness is renewed.
whenever a major modification is carried out.

questions.questionText: Consider a conventional aircraft with three wheels. The nose jack is located 161 inches aft of the datum. The main wheel jacks are located 775 inches aft of the datum. After weighing the following results are reported: nose jack: 2 300 lb; each main wheel jack: 19 300 lb. What is the basic empty mass of the aircraft and the centre of gravity from the datum?
To measure the mass and CG-position of an aircraft, it should be weighed with a minimum of:
Refer to figure 031-12. Where is the centre of gravity of the aeroplane in the diagram given: Nose wheel weight: 1 750 N; Left main wheel weight: 4 050 N; Right main wheel weight: 4 080 N.
When preparing to carry out the weighing procedure on an aircraft, which of the following is not required?
options: Removable passenger services equipment to be off-loaded.
Drain all chemical toilet fluid tanks.

questions.questionText: Consider a conventional aircraft with three wheels. The nose jack is located 161 inches aft of the datum. The main wheel jacks are located 775 inches aft of the datum. After weighing the following results are reported: nose jack: 6 488 lb; each main wheel jack: 17 783 lb. What is the basic empty mass of the aircraft and the centre of gravity from the datum?
An aircraft is weighed prior to entry into service. Who is responsible for deriving the Dry Operational Mass from the weighed mass by the addition of the operational items?

An aircraft with a two wheeled nose gear and four main wheels rests on the ground with a single nose wheel load of 725 kg and a single main wheel load of 6 000 kg. The distance between the nose wheels and the main wheels is 10 meters. How far is the centre of gravity in front of the main wheels?

An aircraft is weighed and the following recordings are made: Nose wheel assembly scale: 5 330 kg; Left main wheel assembly scale: 12 370 kg; Right main wheel assembly scale: 12 480 kg. If the operational items amount to a mass of 1 780 kg with a crew mass of 545 kg, the Empty Mass, as entered in the weight schedule, is:
The basic empty mass of an aeroplane is recorded in:
options: the weighing schedule. If changes occur, due to modifications, the aeroplane must be re-weighed always.
the weighing schedule and is amended to take account of changes due to modifications of the aeroplane.
the loading manifest. It differs from dry operating mass by the value of the "Traffic Load".
the loading manifest. It differs from the zero fuel mass by the value of the "Traffic Load".

questions.questionText: Usually in mass and balance calculations an index is used. The loaded index is:
options: a value of moment which is simplified and scaled down.
used in fuel density calculations and is called fuel-loading index.
used for ground handling purposes and is called wing-loading index.
called floor-loading index and it is used to simplify the loading of an aircraft.

questions.questionText: In mass and balance calculations the "Index" is:
options: a figure without unit of measurement which represents a moment.
an imaginary vertical plane or line from which all measurements are taken.
the range of moments the centre of gravity (CG) can have without making the aeroplane unsafe to fly.
a location in the aeroplane identified by a number.

questions.questionText: Given the following information, calculate the loaded centre of gravity (CG).
The method of using indexes in mass and balance calculations is used to:
options: decrease the magnitude of the useful load.
increase the magnitude of the useful load.
decrease the magnitude of the moments.
increase the magnitude of the moments.

questions.questionText: How can you calculate the DOI?

Cargo compartment 1: empty; Cargo compartment 2: 1 000 kg; Cargo compartment 3: 3 000 kg; Cargo compartment 4: 2 000 kg; Cargo compartment 5: 1 000 kg; Passengers in compartment OA: 20; Passengers in compartment OB: 20; Passengers in compartment OC: 30. The take-off centre of gravity in MAC % (Mean Aerodynamic Chord), will be located at:

Refer to figure 031-56. Last Minute Change (LMC) information: Passengers embarked at row 7: 3 females; Passengers embarked at row 7: 2 males; Baggage loaded in compartment 5: 5 pieces; Cargo box with mail added in compartment 4: 60 kg; Cargo box offloaded from compartment 4: 190 kg. Find the LMC TOTAL value by using standard masses for females, males and baggage for a domestic, scheduled flight:

Refer to figure 031-55. Given: DOI/DOM: 54.0 / 39 000 kg; Passenger load: -2.21 / 10 715 kg; Cargo load: 1.95 / 3 355 kg; Take-off fuel: 11.84 / 8 900 kg. Find the approximate CG position as % MAC for the take-off.

Refer to figure 031-34. The empty mass of your aircraft is 1 100 kg with a CG-position at 3.05 m. The load is as follows: Total Mass of pilot and co-pilot: 150 kg; Total Mass of passengers at rear: 200 kg. In order not to exceed the limitations the minimum remaining fuel on board should be:
Refer to figure 031-10. With respect to a multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the CG location at take-off in the following conditions...
Refer to figure 031-32. From the data contained in the attached appendix, the maximum Allowable Take-off Mass and Traffic Load are respectively:

Refer to figure 031-31. Using the data given in the load and trim sheet, determine which of the following gives the correct values for the Zero Fuel Mass and position of the centre of gravity (% MAC) at that mass.

Refer to figure 031-31. Using the data given in the load and trim sheet, determine from the following the correct values for the Take-off Mass and the position of the centre of gravity at that mass if the fuel index correction to be applied is given as -0.9.
Refer to figure 031-28. Using the load and trim sheet, which of the following is the correct value for the index at a Dry Operating Mass (DOM) of 35 000 kg with a CG at 14% MAC?

Refer to figure 031-45. Without the crew, the mass and longitudinal CG position of the aircraft are 6 000 kg and 4.70 m. The mass of the pilot: 90 kg; The mass of the copilot: 100 kg; The mass of the flight engineer: 80 kg. With the crew, the mass and longitudinal CG position of the aircraft are:
Once the mass and balance documentation has been signed prior to flight:
options: Documented last minute changes to the load may be incorporated.
The documentation is not signed prior to flight.
Acceptable last minute changes to the load must be documented.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 031-45. Without the crew, the weight and the CG position of the aircraft are 7000 kg and 4.70 m. The mass of the pilot: 90 kg; The mass of the copilot: 75 kg; The mass of the flight engineer: 90 kg. With this crew on board, the CG position of the aircraft will be:

Refer to figure 031-29. Using the data given at the appendix to this question, if the fuel index corrections... Which of the following represent the correct values for Landing Mass of the aeroplane and the position of the centre of gravity for this condition?

Refer to figure 031-32. An aeroplane is carrying a Traffic Load of 10320 kg. Complete the necessary sections of the attached appendix and determine which of the answers given below represents the maximum increase in the Traffic Load:

Refer to figure 031-30. From the data given at the appendix and assuming a fuel index shift of -5.7 from the ZFM loaded index, determine which of the following, is the correct value (percentage MAC) for the position of the centre of gravity at Take-off Mass:
Refer to figure 031-10. With respect to a multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the total moment (lbs/in) at landing...
Refer to figure 031-54. Calculate the basic empty mass (BEM) by using the attached loading manifest:
Refer to figure 031-10. With respect to multi-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the Block Fuel moment (lbs.in)...
Refer to figure 031-10. With respect to multi engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the Ramp Mass...
The fuel index:
options: is a standard value given by EASA and can be used for different types of aircraft.
is the difference between the zero fuel mass index and the DOI.
is used to calculate the correct position of the CG due to different locations of the fuel tanks.
is only used for aeroplanes with wing tip tanks.

questions.questionText: Refer to figures 031-35 and 031-38. Just prior to departure, you accept 10 passengers additional on board who will be seated in compartment OC and you have 750 kg unloaded from cargo compartment 5. The take-off centre of gravity in MAC % (Mean Aerodynamic Chord) will be located at:
Refer to figure 031-30. Using the data given at the appendix determine which of the following correctly gives the values of the Zero Fuel Mass (ZFM) of the aeroplane and the load index at ZFM:
Refer to figures 031-01, 031-06, 031-07, 031-09. With respect to a single-engine piston powered aeroplane, determine the Zero Fuel Moment (lbs.in/100)...

Refer to figure 031-33. For the purpose of calculating Traffic Loads... Standard mass values: Male 88 kg, Female 70 kg, Child 35 kg, Infant 6 kg, Hold baggage 14 kg/pcs... select the correct value for the mass of freight which constitutes the remainder of the Traffic Load:
options: no cargo can be loaded in hold number 1.

questions.questionText: Refer to figure 031-57. Calculate the CG position and moment for the BASIC EMPTY MASS by using the data given from the attached table:
options: 115.7 in aft of datum and the moment is 365 074 in.lb
95.3 in aft of datum and the moment is 380 373 in.lb
113.6 in aft of datum and the moment is 430 998 in.lb
111.4 in aft of datum and the moment is 489 666 in.lb

questions.questionText: Given: Total Mass: 2 900 kg; Centre of gravity (CG) location: 115.0; Aft CG limit station: 116.0. The maximum mass that can be added at station 130.0 is:

Given: Actual mass: 116 500 lbs; Original CG station: 435.0; Compartment A station: 285.5; Compartment B station: 792.5. If 390 lbs of cargo is moved from compartment B (aft) to compartment A (forward), what is the station number of the new CG?

Given: Total Mass: 7 500 kg; Centre of gravity (CG) location station: 80.5; Aft CG limit station: 79.5. How much cargo must be shifted from the aft cargo compartment at station 150 to the forward cargo compartment at station 30 in order to move the CG location to the aft limit?

An aircraft has a mass of 5 000 lbs and the CG is located at 80 in aft of the datum. The aft CG limit is at 80.5 in aft of the datum. What is the maximum mass that can be loaded into a hold situated 150 in aft of the datum without exceeding the limit?
Assume: Aeroplane gross mass: 4 750 kg; Centre of gravity at station: 115.8. If 100 kg is moved from station 30 to station 120 the new position of the centre of gravity will be at station:
Refer to figure 032-23. assuming constant L/D ratio, which of the diagrams provided correctly shows the movement of the THRUST REQUIRED CURVE? Mass M1 is higher than mass M2.

Refer to figure 032-17. Consider the graphic representation of the power required versus true air speed (T°S), for a piston engined aeroplane with a given mass. When drawing the tangent from the origin, the point of contact (°) determines the speed of:
Refer to figure 032-22. Which of the following diagrams correctly shows the movement of the power required curve with increasing altitude (H1 < H2)?
Refer to figure 032-24. Considering a rate of climb diagram (ROC versus T°S) for an aeroplane. Which of the diagrams shows the correct curves for FL°PS DOWN compared to CLE°N configuration?
Refer to figure 032-41. Choose a correct statement below.
options: Point D: the aircraft would decelerate if it would fly straight and level
The speed at point C would result in a greater angle of descent than at point B.
Point F: maximum rate of descent
Point C: maximum angle of climb

questions.questionText: Which of the jet engine ratings below is not a certified rating?
°n aeroplane operating under the 180 minutes ETOPS rule may be up to:
options: 180 minutes flying time to a suitable airport in still air with one engine inoperative.
180 minutes flying time to a suitable airport under the prevailing weather condition with one engine inoperative.
60 minutes flying time in still air with both engines operating at the normal cruising speed.
180 minutes flying time from suitable airport in still air with both engines operating at a normal cruising speed.

questions.questionText: What will be the effect on an aeroplane performance if aerodrome pressure altitude is decreased?
options: It will increase the take-off ground run.
It will decrease the take-off distance required.
It will increase the take-off distance required.
It will increase the accelerate stop distance.

questions.questionText: With all engines out, a pilot wants to fly for maximum time. Therefore he has to fly the speed corresponding to:
Uphill slope:
options: increases the take-off distance more than the accelerate stop distance.
decreases the accelerate stop distance only.

questions.questionText: How do the best angle of climb and best rate of climb vary with increasing altitude for an aeroplane with a normal aspirated piston engine?
options: Best angle of climb increases while best rate of climb decreases.
Best angle of climb decreases while best rate of climb increases.

questions.questionText: What is true about a clearway?
options: ° clearway can be water or land and must be under the control of the aerodrome authority.
° clearway can be used to bring the aircraft to a full stop after an engine failure.
° clearway has a minimum width of 90 m.
° clearway must have an imaginary gradient of 1.5 %.

questions.questionText: Explain the difference, if any, between the effect of a tailwind on the flight path angle and the effect of a tailwind on the descent angle:
options: ° tailwind will decrease the flight path angle and the descent angle will remain constant.
° tailwind will decrease the flight path angle and decrease the descent angle.
° tailwind will increase the flight path angle and decrease the descent angle.
In a tailwind, the flight path angle will remain constant and the descent angle will remain constant.

questions.questionText: ° decrease in atmospheric pressure has, among other things, the following consequences on take-off performance:
options: a reduced take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.
an increased take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.
a reduced take-off distance and improved initial climb performance.
an increased take-off distance and degraded initial climb performance.

questions.questionText: Which statement is correct for a descent without engine thrust at maximum lift to drag ratio speed?
options: The higher the average temperature (O°T) the lower is the speed for descent.
The mass of an aeroplane does not have any effect on the speed for descent.
The higher the gross mass the lower is the speed for descent.
The higher the gross mass the greater is the speed for descent.

questions.questionText: During climb to the cruising level, a headwind component:
options: decreases the ground distance flown during that climb.
increases the amount of fuel for the climb.

questions.questionText: What happens to the performance of an aircraft if the QNH is low?
options: The landing performance decreases and the go-around performance increases.
The take-off performance increases due to higher engine thrust.
The engine thrust decreases due to a lower air density.

questions.questionText: On a reciprocating engine aeroplane, to maintain a given angle of attack, configuration and altitude at higher gross mass:
options: an increase in airspeed and power is required.
requires an increase in power and decrease in the airspeed.
an increase in airspeed is required but power setting does not change.
a higher coefficient of drag is required.

questions.questionText: Considering T°S for maximum range and maximum endurance, other factors remaining constant:
options: T°S for maximum range will increase with increased altitude while T°S for maximum endurance will decrease with increased altitude.
both will stay constant regardless of altitude.
both will decrease with increasing altitude.
both will increase with increasing altitude.

questions.questionText: °n uphill slope:
How does the lift coefficient for maximum range vary with altitude? (No compressibility effects.)
options: Only at low speeds the lift coefficient decreases with increasing altitude.
The lift coefficient decreases with increasing altitude.
The lift coefficient is independent of altitude.
The lift coefficient increases with increasing altitude.

questions.questionText: The lowest point of the drag or thrust required curve of a jet aeroplane, respectively, is the point for:
Which force compensates the weight in unaccelerated straight and level flight?
options: The resultant from lift and drag.

questions.questionText: In a steady descending flight (descent angle G°MM°) equilibrium of forces acting on the aeroplane is given by:
The intersections of the thrust available and the drag curve are the operating points of the aeroplane:
What is the effect of tailwind on the time to climb to a given altitude?
options: The time to climb does not change.
The effect on time to climb will depend on the aeroplane type.
